title: 'Dune: Part Two' and the 10 Best Movies of the Year … So Far
format: A Q1 stocktaking, recorded during eclipse week. Sean and Amanda argue about which movie quarter matters, agree Dune is the film of the year so far, then run through the gems hiding in the first-quarter rough. Plus a read of a genuinely deranged Netflix movie chart and Sean's interview with First Omen director Arkasha Stevenson.

segments.heading: Is it too early for a best-of-the-year list?
segments.note: the one-week Oscar-qualifying runs that make 2024 feel like 2023

films.title: The Promised Land
films.note: Nikolaj Arcel's film, one of several they'd already covered with a director interview before it got a February wide release.

films.title: Perfect Days
films.note: The Wim Wenders movie, banking on an International Feature nomination it got (the kind of boost that can matter for the box office).

films.title: Love Lies Bleeding
films.note: They gave it a whole episode. Good in March, would have been good in September.

films.title: Immaculate
films.note: The Sydney Sweeney nunsploitation horror movie Sean liked a lot, and, weirdly, the near-twin of The First Omen two weeks later.

films.title: The Zone of Interest
films.note: So dominant in the International Feature race for so long that the qualifying-run gamble barely mattered for it.

films.title: The Taste of Things
films.note: Amanda's call that Sean keeps forgetting came out this year; banked on a nomination that didn't come.

films.title: Society of the Snow
films.note: The one they both liked that actually came out in January and gets filed, unfairly, under last year.

segments.heading: The big one
segments.note: Sean's favorite movie of the year so far, on a third theatrical viewing

films.title: Dune: Part Two
films.note: A breathless act of filmmaking that, on rewatch, reads as actively anti-Paul, an epic that distrusts its own protagonist the way Taxi Driver does. Sean credits the Villeneuve-Spielberg DGA podcast for the frame: Paul isn't the chosen one, he's manufactured into the role. Well cast against Chalamet's winsome public persona. With Villeneuve making a nuclear-war movie next, Dune Messiah (mostly palace intrigue, less spectacle) is probably five years out.

segments.heading: Gems in the rough
segments.note: the good stuff Sean dug out of Q1

films.title: La Chimera
films.note: Alice Rohrwacher's 'emo Indiana Jones,' with Josh O'Connor in one white linen suit holding a twig and looking sad while he hunts Etruscan remains and a lost love. A mood piece; the best scene is O'Connor eating pasta while 18 Italian women, Isabella Rossellini among them, yell at each other.

films.title: The First Omen
films.note: A legacy prequel that plays as a genuine David Lynch / Cronenberg body-horror film. Disney released it; Sean can't believe it isn't NC-17. Ambient and upsetting rather than jump-scare city, and ultimately about control of a woman's body, the same nerve as Immaculate.

films.title: Girls State
films.note: The Boys State follow-up, on Apple TV+, shot in 2022 Missouri right as the Roe leak landed. The reactions were muted and Amanda's was too, but the fury at how the girls are treated versus the boys is the animating thread, and Emily Worthmore, who loses but does the journalism, is the standout.

films.title: How to Have Sex
films.note: Molly Manning Walker's debut, on MUBI: 'feel-bad British Spring Breakers.' Verité focus on Mia McKenna-Bruce's teenager on an island vacation, whose walk of shame becomes a single, devastating still shot. An affecting, hard watch.

films.title: Spermworld
films.note: Lance Oppenheim's Hulu documentary (Sean's favorite documentarian of the last five years) on the unregulated forums where sperm donors find hopeful parents. Stylized verité portraits of four odd, not-bad men, rooted in Nelly Bowles's Times piece 'The Sperm Kings Have a Problem.'

films.title: The Beast
films.note: Bertrand Bonello's Lynchian, time-hopping film with Léa Seydoux purifying her DNA across 1910, 2014 and 2044 and George MacKay as her lover. Confusing and uneven, but the 2014 incel-stalker segment is the best part (some think it should have been the whole movie), and the last 20 minutes are exceptional.

films.title: Snack Shack
films.note: A T Street (Rian Johnson's company) throwback to '80s/'90s teen comedy, set in 1991 Nebraska, on VOD. Two kids take over the local pool's snack shack. Riddled with cliché and knows it; the reason to watch is the insane cut-and-perform energy, plus Gabriel LaBelle as a star.

films.title: Scoop
films.note: A Netflix newsroom procedural about the 2019 Prince Andrew Newsnight interview, told from the booker's side (Billie Piper). Rufus Sewell and Gillian Anderson are riveting in the climactic sit-down. The good version of what season six of The Crown botched: thin, but exactly the serious-not-Serious movie Sean wishes Netflix made more of.

films.title: The People's Joker
films.note: Vera Drew's crowdfunded public-domain parody of very-much-not-public-domain IP: a coming-out story built from DC characters that most assumed would never get released for legal reasons. Bold, occasionally tedious, sometimes thrilling; Richard Brody called it the greatest superhero movie he's ever seen.

segments.heading: What America was actually watching
segments.note: the genuinely chaotic Netflix movie Top 10 that week
segments.subject: Netflix's movie Top 10

films.title: The Little Things
films.note: No. 1: the Denzel/Malek/Leto serial-killer movie that went day-and-date on HBO Max during COVID, now topping Netflix.

films.title: Happy Gilmore
films.note: No. 2: 'five-star classic.'

films.title: Glass
films.note: No. 3: the M. Night Shyamalan Unbreakable sequel.

films.title: Baby Driver
films.note: No. 4: recently added, a movie Sean likes very much.

films.title: The Tearsmith
films.note: No. 5: the lone other Netflix original nobody had heard of; a teen drama, no supernatural element despite the title.

films.title: Scoop
films.note: No. 6: the one they'd just spent ten minutes on.

films.title: Space Jam: A New Legacy
films.note: No. 7: 'a dreadful film. Congratulations to Warner Bros.'

films.title: Skyscraper
films.note: No. 8: The Rock, which triggered Amanda's guessing game.

films.title: Split
films.note: No. 9: more McAvoy, more Shyamalan.

films.title: The Super Mario Bros. Movie
films.note: No. 10: 'What the hell, man?'

films.title: The First Omen
films.note: The photojournalist-turned-AFI-director sold the studio on the movie with its single most provocative image: the frontal childbirth shot that fought the MPA over an NC-17 (the vagina, not the demon hand protruding from it, was the problem). She frames the '70s wave (Omen, Exorcist, Rosemary's Baby) as parents fearing their children, and this one as the inversion: children fearing their parents' generation, pitched the day of Texas's six-week ban. Nell Tiger-Free carries almost every frame.

films.title: Hollywood 90028
films.note: Her 'last great thing': a recently Grindhouse-restored, hyper-feminist horror film shot through a male perspective about the Hollywood strangler, with an ending shot so bold it made her angry.

films: Close Encounters of the Third Kind

referenced.group: What they actually watched this weekend
